headline: Lady of the Rockies Declared 'Mission Accomplished', 1985
body:
The wind, a ceaseless sculptor of the Montana peaks, had long held dominion over the East Ridge, a stark, unforgiving sentinel above Butte. But on December 20, 1985, a new force, born of human will and communal grit, claimed its own victory. The Montana Standard, with a headline that cut through the winter air like a steel blade, declared, "Lady Mission Accomplished." It was a simple phrase, yet it carried the weight of six years of relentless toil, a testament to what men and women, bound by a shared vision, could wrench from the unyielding earth.

The genesis of this colossal undertaking lay not in grand civic planning, but in the quiet desperation of one man, Bob O'Bill, a Butte mine electrician. In 1979, as his wife, Joyce, battled cancer, O'Bill made a solemn vow: if she recovered, he would erect a statue of the Virgin Mary. Joyce did recover, and O'Bill, a man of his word, began to move mountains, metaphorically and almost literally. His initial vision, a modest five-foot figure, quickly swelled, fueled by the fervent spirit of a community that understood struggle and resilience. The project evolved, broadening its embrace to honor all women, particularly mothers, transforming a personal pledge into a universal tribute.

The task was Herculean. The chosen site, perched at 8,500 feet on the Continental Divide, was a brutal canvas of rock and sky. There were no roads, no easy access, only the raw, elemental challenge of the high country. But Butte, a city forged in the crucible of copper mining, knew how to dig, how to build, how to endure. Volunteers, men and women from all walks of life, answered the call. They cleared land, blasted rock, and carved a winding, treacherous road up the mountainside. Every ounce of material, every tool, every drop of water had to be hauled up that arduous path.

Leroy Lee, a skilled welder and friend of O'Bill, took on the monumental task of sculpting the Lady. He worked with steel, transforming cold metal into the flowing robes and serene countenance of the 90-foot figure. The statue was too vast to be built in one piece; it was fabricated in sections, each a formidable challenge in itself. Imagine the clang of hammers, the hiss of welding torches, the sweat and strain of men wrestling with tons of steel, all against the backdrop of Montana's fierce weather. Winter brought blizzards that buried the site in feet of snow, summer brought scorching sun and sudden, violent thunderstorms. Yet, the work pressed on, driven by an almost primal urge to see the vision realized.

Then came the final, audacious act: the assembly. The Nevada Air National Guard, with their specialized sky crane, became an indispensable ally. Piece by piece, the massive sections, some weighing as much as 60 tons, were airlifted from the valley floor, swaying precariously against the vast expanse of the sky. On that crisp December day in 1985, as the last section, the Lady's head, was gently lowered into place, a collective breath was held across Butte. The moment it settled, perfectly aligned, marked not just the completion of a statue, but the culmination of an extraordinary human endeavor. The Lady of the Rockies now stood, a silent, majestic guardian, her gaze sweeping across the vast, rugged landscape, a testament to the enduring power of faith, community, and sheer, unadulterated will.
